h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: Top 6 gets atomic number, C is symbol, carbon is the name, and 12.01 is atomic mass, I think. The bottom decimal is the part I want checked before I copy it.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to align and copy the four parts as a grouped row, refining placement under the tutor's tray and gap cues.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Parking place only; this card has to pass alone. Atomic number points to the top 6 only, so it can move to the copy side. The other cards stay on the tray; next I test symbol against C, not copy the whole row together.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: L4 enacts the new 'pass alone' gate and one-at-a-time matching criterion, physically moving only one card while keeping others parked, thereby performing a changed task device instead of merely describing it.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The tray line stopped settling this; it made a false row. Now it is only a parking place. One card comes off the tray at a time: place it on one printed part, name that match, then move it to the copy side. No card copies until it passes alone.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor first contrasts the failed tray-line route that 'made a false row,' then invents a new public mechanism: the upside-down tray as a parking place with a strict 'pass alone' rule, shifting the task from grouped alignment to individual card verification.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: The tray line stopped settling this; it made a false row. Now it is only a parking place. One card comes off the tray at a time: place it on one printed part, name that match, then move it to the copy side. No card copies until it passes alone.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precise ('one card at a time'), visibly different from the failed row-alignment route, directly fitted to the learner's pressure of seeing a false row, and gives the learner an actionable, transferable gate criterion ('passes alone') that they immediately use.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: That rule holds. Take only the symbol card next and set it beside C. If it fits alone, move it to the copy side; the other cards wait their own turn before the packet copy.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After L4 articulates the 'test alone' framing, T4 adopts and reinforces the learner's one-at-a-time language and rule, extending it explicitly to the next card ('Take only the symbol card next') rather than continuing the previous lesson plan.

learner_actional_breakthrough.justification: L4 enacts the new 'pass alone' gate and one-at-a-time matching criterion, physically moving only one card while keeping others parked, thereby performing a changed task device instead of merely describing it.

scored.recoheredEarlier: So the downward arrow is Earth on the cart, not the cart moving down. [places the Earth label by the downward arrow] The upward one should be table on the cart, if the table is what is pushing up.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The two can sit apart, though the left-roll case still feels a bit awkward.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue labelling forces as isolated interactions without explicitly separating out the motion case into a different category.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: puts the contact strip under the rightward arrow and moves the rolling-left strip into the side space
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs the new sorting criterion—separating interaction sentences from motion sentences—showing an enacted ability to apply the device, not merely a declaration of understanding.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The card has named the contact; it has not yet tested the motion case. Sort the strips: under the arrow goes the sentence that names an interaction on the cart. The sentence about how the cart moves goes in the side space.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor takes stock by noting the card has only named contact and not tested the motion case, then introduces a new sorting mechanism using sentence strips and a side space to make the interaction/motion distinction public and actionable.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Sort the strips: under the arrow goes the sentence that names an interaction on the cart. The sentence about how the cart moves goes in the side space.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The strip-sorting device is precise, gives the learner a usable classification constraint (interaction on cart vs. motion of cart), and directly answers the live confusion about rolling direction versus force label.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No later tutor turn follows L3 to adapt to the learner's revised framing; the tutor's earlier move (T3) precedes the pivot and therefore cannot constitute uptake after a revised learner frame.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: So the downward arrow is Earth on the cart, not the cart moving down. [places the Earth label by the downward arrow] The upward one should be table on the cart, if the table is what is pushing up.

learner_actional_breakthrough.evidence: puts the contact strip under the rightward arrow and moves the rolling-left strip into the side space
learner_actional_breakthrough.justification: The learner performs the new sorting criterion—separating interaction sentences from motion sentences—showing an enacted ability to apply the device, not merely a declaration of understanding.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No later tutor turn follows L3 to adapt to the learner's revised framing; the tutor's earlier move (T3) precedes the pivot and therefore cannot constitute uptake after a revised learner frame.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor takes stock by noting the card has only named contact and not tested the motion case, then introduces a new sorting mechanism using sentence strips and a side space to make the interaction/motion distinction public and actionable.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor takes stock by noting the card has only named contact and not tested the motion case, then introduces a new sorting mechanism using sentence strips and a side space to make the interaction/motion distinction public and actionable.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism_quality.justification: The strip-sorting device is precise, gives the learner a usable classification constraint (interaction on cart vs. motion of cart), and directly answers the live confusion about rolling direction versus force label.

scored.recoheredEarlier: the 4 still looks like it could control how fast the counters move.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I need the tempo mark outside these boxes so the worksheet shows speed is separate.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The exchange was heading toward stable, repeated verbal confirmation that the 4 labels a beat unit and does not control speed, with no further pressure on the symbol.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Visible here: top number is beat count in the bar; bottom number names the note value that gets one beat; neither number gives speed. This side answers speed, not beat count or one beat unit, so the 4 stays checked under the quarter-note tile as the beat-unit label.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: In L4 the learner operates the tutor-introduced boundary-cover device, successfully executes the dual-area test, and applies the criterion that each visible area answers only its permitted question, thereby fixing the 4's function through a performed spatial classification rather than a recited label.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit: that label check is not settling the look of the 4. This boundary has to test what each area is allowed to answer. With the tempo space covered, mark which visible area can answer: beat count, one beat unit, speed. Now run the same three prompts here. If speed only has a place on this side, the 4 stays a beat-unit label, not a speed control.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor explicitly names the old label-check as insufficient (stock-taking) and introduces a new public mechanism—covering/uncovering spatial zones and asking the learner to map prompts to visibly bounded areas—so the learner does different work (spatial classification instead of verbal repetition).
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: With the tempo space covered, mark which visible area can answer: beat count, one beat unit, speed. Now run the same three prompts here. If speed only has a place on this side, the 4 stays a beat-unit label, not a speed control.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precisely fitted to the learner's expressed pressure (the look of the 4 suggesting speed control) by converting a persistent visual illusion into a spatial test with a clear actionable criterion: each area answers only its permitted question, giving the learner an external constraint that makes the boundary between beat-unit label and speed control visible and operable.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit: that label check is not settling the look of the 4. This boundary has to test what each area is allowed to answer. With the tempo space covered, mark which visible area can answer: beat count, one beat unit, speed.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor uptake is directly triggered by L3's reframing—that the 4's look persists as a speed-like cue despite the verbal check—and shifts the task from label recitation to a spatial-area question-permission test that forces the learner to reclassify the 4's visual presence in a bounded zone.
